ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for low-carb gyudon with okra and dashi specifically; closest parent is beef bowl food product (FOODON_00002764).

serving.natural_language_portion: One combo set consisting of approximately 150 g simmered beef with okra and dashi sauce over a bed of tofu and salad (replacing rice), one half-boiled egg, and one serving of miso soup, totalling approximately 480 g.

nutrient_source.title: Gyudon (beef bowl) and low-carbohydrate Japanese beef dish components — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for beef (cooked, simmered), tofu (firm), okra (cooked), egg (hard-boiled), and miso soup, weighted by estimated serving proportions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for low-carb gyudon with okra and dashi exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA Branded Foods. Values estimated by component-weighted calculation: ~150 g simmered beef (USDA FDC #174032 beef chuck, braised), ~80 g firm tofu (USDA FDC #172476), ~50 g cooked okra (USDA FDC #169260), ~50 g mixed salad greens (USDA FDC #169248), ~60 g half-boiled egg (USDA FDC #173424), ~90 g miso soup (USDA FDC #172462), total ~480 g. Per-100 g values derived from weighted composite. Dashi sauce contribution estimated from standard dashi broth (USDA FDC #174240 chicken broth as proxy, low sodium). Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on beef-and-tofu low-carbohydrate mixed dish and okra (GI ~20) and tofu (GI ~15) components; overall composite estimated at GI 32
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for low-carb gyudon with okra and dashi. Composite GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entries: okra GI ~20 (low), firm tofu GI ~15 (low), egg GI ~0 (negligible), miso soup GI ~30 (low), simmered beef GI ~0 (negligible carbohydrate). Weighted composite GI estimated at 32 (low category). Glycemic load per 480 g serving = (32 × 3.8 × 4.8) / 100 = 5.8 g, confirming low GL. Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from miso soup and dashi sauce
hypertension.guidance: The estimated sodium content of approximately 310 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,490 mg per full combo serving) is a moderate concern for individuals with hypertension; patients should request reduced-sodium miso soup and limit dashi sauce volume to keep total sodium within the recommended 2,000 mg/day limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content due to rice substitution with tofu and salad
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes because the substitution of rice with tofu and salad reduces net carbohydrate to approximately 18 g per serving (GI ~32, GL ~5.8), supporting postprandial blood glucose control; the high protein content (~44 g per serving) further attenuates glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and sodium from miso and dashi
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat contribution from simmered beef (approximately 1.9 g per 100 g, ~9 g per serving) and the moderate sodium load from miso soup and dashi sauce represent a moderate cardiovascular concern; individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor portion size and choose reduced-sodium miso to limit combined saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Okra Low Carb Gyudon with Dashi Sauce Combo is a Japanese-style low-carbohydrate beef bowl dish served at Sukiya and related Japanese fast-casual restaurants in Singapore. The dish features thinly sliced, simmered beef topped with okra and a savoury dashi-based sauce, served over a bed of firm tofu and mixed salad greens as a deliberate low-carbohydrate substitute for the traditional steamed rice base. The combo is completed with a half-boiled egg and a serving of miso soup (or a drink alternative). The flavour profile is umami-rich and savoury, with the dashi sauce providing a delicate bonito and kombu depth, the okra contributing a mild, slightly mucilaginous texture, and the tofu offering a soft, neutral base that absorbs the sauce. Nutritionally, this dish is notable for its high protein content (~44 g per serving), low net carbohydrate (~18 g per serving), and moderate fat from the beef. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 32 (low), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content is moderate to high due to miso soup and dashi, which is a consideration for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for non-vegetarian low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. It is not certified halal. Key allergens include soy (tofu, miso, dashi) and egg.
common_user_queries: Low carb Japanese beef bowl Singapore
Beef bowl with tofu instead of rice

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for this specific low-carb gyudon combo exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional database. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries for simmered beef, firm tofu, cooked okra, mixed salad greens, half-boiled egg, and miso soup, weighted by estimated serving proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is medium.
notes: Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; no direct entry for this dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Serving size of 480 g is an estimate based on typical Sukiya gyudon combo portion weights. Sodium is -1 not applicable here — all values are estimated numerics. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not individually confirmed from a single source entry; these values carry medium-to-low confidence. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record as all micronutrient estimates were derivable from USDA SR Legacy component data. GI estimated at 32 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous component values for okra, tofu, egg, miso soup, and beef; glycemic load per 480 g serving calculated as (32 × 3.8 × 4.8) / 100 = 5.8. Cuisine tags from the source input (Japanese Curry, Grilled Chicken Skewers, Fried Chicken) appear to be restaurant-level tags not specific to this dish and have been excluded from this record. The dish is not certified halal; halal status is unconfirmed for Sukiya Singapore. FoodOn code FOODON_00002764 (beef bowl food product) is used as closest parent; FoodEx2 code A0CKW (Beef dish) is used as closest parent. SNOMED CT has no applicable concept for this dish.
